sentences.text: My name is Jean Litz, J-E-A-N, L-I-T-Z.
Genia Kozak.
Sure, G-E-N-I-A K-O-Z-A-K.
No.
March 3, 1928.
I was born in Mlynow, Poland.
M-L-Y-N-O-W.
Dubno, Rovno, Lutsk.
They were like the three bigger towns of Mlynow.
My mother's name was Eva Kozak.
And my father's name was Icek Kozak.
They were wonderful people, very hard-working, wonderful, wonderful people.
Good-natured, nice people.
He had a store that he sold coal to the farmers, white wash, and we also had horses, transporting goods from one town to the other.
Yes.
I had three brothers and one sister.
One of my brothers passed away when I was nine years old, before the war, you know.
And right now, I have two brothers and one sister.
Kalman, Kalman, Kalman Kozak.
My eldest brother is Reuben Kozak, and then is Morris, and me, and then my sister.
Her name is Karen Lowenthal.
It was-- Mlynow was very small town.
It was very nice.
We were children, young children.
We had a happy childhood.
Our childhood was happy.
And really no complaints.
It was a happy home, you know, happy home.
In the summertime, we went swimming.
We had a beautiful lake in our town.
And we used to go swimming.
That was wonderful.
And we used to ride on my father's wagons when they had to pick the hay.
You could bring back the hay to the stables.
That was fun.
You know, it was very leisurely, a very nice, leisure, quiet life.
Oh, sure.
You know, sure.
Lots of friends, lots of friends.
We would go to school, and come home, and my mother would ask us to go to the store for errand, you know, very, very normal.
The wintertime was wonderful.
The winters were horrible cold.
But my brothers used to take me and my sister sledding a lot-- we used to-- you know, we had a lot of fun.
It was fun.
It was a fun-- it was a hard life.
It was a hard life on my parents, but it was a fun life for the children.
It was fun.
We went to public school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if you would call it a Catholic school, but a cross was always there, and all the teachers were Polish.
It was not a religious school, but it was definitely a Catholic public school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Our religious life in-- in Mlynow was we were very religious.
We didn't have-- Orthodox and reform.
We had-- we were all Orthodox.
We all ate kosher food, and we all went to shul.
Every Shabbos, the holidays, of course, very religious.
Everybody was religious.
Yes.
But they didn't have bar mitzvahs like they have here, big affairs.
They're in the synagogue.
You know, they bring in a couple, you know, bottles of wine or whiskey, whatever, with all the-- with all the-- the rabbi and all the Jews, you know, they-- and cake.
That was-- that was a bar mitzvah in Europe.
All of my friends were Jewish.
All of my friends were Jewish.
Of course.
Walking from school one day in the wintertime, one boy threw a snowball and made my forehead, and he hollered, [NON-ENGLISH], the Jews to Palestine.
And I came home crying that day.
And there were many other, sure, there was always antisemitism, always, always.
It was in September.
I think it was September the 1st.
I was 13 years old.
I was a child, I was a young kid.
We heard these horrible explosions, and we didn't know what was happening.
And it was the Germans, the Luftwaffe was bombing.
We had an airport in our town, and they bombed all the planes, all the planes.
And a couple days later, they marched in, a day later.
I don't remember.
I was a kid.
We-- we couldn't understand what-- what-- what it is.
But they marched in a couple days later.
[SIGHS] Well, we were watching them, and they were watching us.

And-- I remember a couple days later they would march in, in the houses, and take out silverware and whatever they wanted, whatever they liked, they just helped themselves, and just took it with them.
Beautiful, beautiful it was September.
It was beautiful.
It was beautiful.
I remember that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
No.
I remember when they came in.
They threw us out of the house, and they made our house for the headquarters.
So I remember one in particular, German.
His name was Schwartz.
He was mean.
He was so mean.
My sister had a little cat.
She always played with the cat.
And one day he came out to clean his revolver.
And he said to my sister, put the cat down.
And my sister just hugged the cat.
And I tell her, I says, you better not risk-- put the-- put the cat down.
And she did.
And he took two shots at her.
And she lived.
The cat lived.
He took two shots at her hind legs.
He aimed at her hind leg.
And me and my sister took it in to the stable.
We lived in the stables of that time, because they took over the house.
And we tore a sheet, and we wrapped up the cat.
And I don't know what happened after that.
That-- him, I remember in particular, as he was-- was vicious.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
In the stables, like a stable.
On one side there were horses and the cows, and-- and the other side we had-- thank god we had floors and electricity.
So we just stayed there, you know.
Well, the first winter was very bad, I remember.
We didn't have-- we had a little-- what you would call an oven or something-- to keep us warm.
And I used to go with my sister in the woods to pick wood.
We used to get-- chop these trees and wood.
And one day I brought the wood back.
And I tried-- I don't know where the men were.
I think they were working for the Germans, my brothers and my father.
And I chopped a tree to get some wood to burn in the house.
And a piece of wood hit my forehead.
I still have the scar today.
And it opened up my-- my skin.
And I was in such agony.
I was in such pain.
And there was no hospital.
We were not allowed to go to no hospital.
And somebody, a neighbor or somebody, put iodine.
Well, the screams-- [LAUGHS] it was rough.
It was rough.
It was rough.
That was not in the ghetto.
That was before the ghetto.
That was before the ghetto.
And then one day they-- they closed, our house fell into the ghetto.
It was a small town.
We were only 2,000 Jews.
They made all the Jews move.
We were-- the Jews were like spread out.
They made us move to a few blocks in the town.
And our house was-- happened to be in the ghetto.
And we managed.
In fact, the first Passover, I don't know.
I think my father got flour from-- see my father, my father used to drive for a man that was a German collaborator.
And his name was Sawitsky.
He was a-- he was a cruel man.
Sawitsky, I would say S-A-W-I-T-S-K-Y, you know.
My father worked for him.
My father drove the horses for him.
He was a nobody before the war.
But when the Germans came in, he became a somebody.
And my father managed to bring in flour to the ghetto.
And they baked matzahs real quietly in our house before Passover.
I remember that.
And we as kids, I thought it was fun, you know.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was-- it was bad.
It was, you know, the winters were very, very cold, very.
Then the summer came.
And my father didn't want me to stay in the ghetto.
He wanted me to go work on a farm.
He said, you'll have fresh air, and you'll have food.
And-- but he also told me when somebody comes to tell you to leave, don't go to the ghetto, but come to the stables where I keep the horses, [BACKROUND NOISE] where he was driving.
So, I worked.
My father got this Sokoluk.
His name was Sokoluk.
He was a very well-to-do farmer.
Could you spell Sokoluk's name?
I would say S-O-K-O-L-U-K, Sokoluk.
He was not a bad man.
He was not a bad man.
He took-- well, he took me in to work, I mean, for nothing.
I-- I worked hard.
I used to take the cows out to pasture like 4:00 or 5:00 in the morning, barefooted.
It was cold.
Even in the summer, I remember it was cold.
But I worked.
And I-- I was there quite a bit.
And then one day, just like my father warned me, there was a Ukrainian policeman, a collaborator with the Germans came.
And he said, are you Genia Kozak?
And I said, yeah.
And he says, you have to go back to the ghetto.
I said, of course, I'll go back to the ghetto.
But I knew.
And instead of going to the ghetto, I went to this little town, Morawica.
And I slept there.
I went into one of the Jewish homes there.
They knew me.
They knew my father.
And I stayed with them a couple hours.
And then at 4 o'clock in the morning I walked-- I walked from there to the stables.
I can't remember.
It must have been a linen dress like-- you know, like the-- the farmers' daughters would wear.
And I came back to the stables, and my father had my mother already.
He took her out from the ghetto, and my sister.
My sister and my mother were there.
And I came in.
They were very happy to see me.
And my father says, I'm gonna take you over to-- a week before, my father found out that they were going to kill all the Jews in our town.
Because he drove-- at that time he drove some Germans.
But they didn't know that my father was Jewish.
And he overheard them.
And as he was driving into the ghetto, he saw the graves were being dug.
And he knew that that was the end.
And my father came back to the ghetto.
And he called some of his friends, Jews, and he says, run.
Run for-- if you can, run for your life.
Because I saw them dig the graves.
[SIGHS] That was 1942, I think.
I think.
I'm not good with dates.
Because I was a kid.
I was a child.
But my father put me and my sister in a wagon and put the hay over it, and a seat over it, and he drove to Unubra.
This was the man that a week before told my father-- my father ran around looking for a place to hide.
And nobody wanted to take him.
And some farmers said, we'll take you.
Because we know you.
We'll take you with the boys, but not the girls and your wife.
And he was the last chance, this Unubra Cherashuk.
He was a saint.
C-H-E-R-E-S-H-U-K, Cherashuk.
My father knew him.
He knew his parents.
Because they used to come and buy from us.
All the farm-- my father was known all over.
Because you know, they knew him.
They just used to make stops, and sometimes they would even eat in our house, you know, when it was market day.
They all knew him.
And he went to Unubra.
And he cried.
My father broke down and cried.
He says, I've been all over.
I have to save my family.
He says, I have no place else.
You're my last chance.
And he says to my father, you come.
And that's the day my-- he put my sister and me in the wagon, and he put a seat over us.
And he drove out to go to Morawica to-- to-- to where Unubra lived.
And a policeman stops him.
And he says to my father, where are you going?
What's your hurry?
And my father says, I have to run an errand for Sawitsky.
That was that collaborator he was working for.
Oh, he says, if we-- if I would have sneezed on my sister, if I coughed, the sneeze would have been the end of us.
But God was with us, and the policemen left.
And my father took us to Unubra's.
And we climbed up on the straw.
And then he brought my mother.
And then he went for my brother.
How my brother Morris got there, I don't know.
My brother Morris, [INAUDIBLE], because he worked in a different town altogether.
Three days later, this was in October.
Because my father has written down on a picture that October the 8th, they killed every Jew in Mlynow.
That was three days later.
They-- we heard every shot.
Because distance-wise, it was near.
To walk, maybe it was a couple kilometers.
But by then, we heard every shot.
And--
My father-- we all knew, sure, we knew.
Because, the children, we knew because of this-- and after that, we couldn't lay on top of the straw.
We had to dig someplace for hiding.
Because we didn't know for how long.
And so next, there was a barn.
It was called a [UKRAINIAN] And next to the barn--
It was called a [UKRAINIAN].
[UKRAINIAN] is Ukrainian for a barn.
And next to the barn was a pigsty.
He had pigs.

And they came up, if they would dig a hole in they barn and cut a board to the pigsty, then when they go feed the pigs, nobody will be suspicious that they carrying food into the barn, which was very good.
And so we lived next to the pigsty.
And he was a very poor farmer.
He had very little.
But he would bring us some potatoes, and-- you know, very little.
And this is how we dug the hole.
And we stayed there for over two years.
It was very small.
We couldn't stand up.
And the first winter was horrible.
We ate nothing but onions, onions, onions.
And from the onions, my two brothers, Morris and Ruby, broke out in welts.
And it covered the whole body, not us, just the men.
Not my father, just the boys, Ruby and Morris.
And they couldn't pass their water.
It was so-- my father said, if we don't do something they'll die.
So I said to my father, I'll go out.
And I'll go-- we had a country doctor.
His name was Fedorowich.
And that's spelled?
F-E-D-O-R-O-W-I-C-H.
It's different there than-- he was a good man.
He was a good man.
I crawled out from the hole.
We had like a long, like a little tunnel made with straw.
We'd go out and cover it back with the straw.
So if you walked in the barn, all you saw was straw.
And I went to him.
And I-- walked in his house.
And I always walked where no humans walked.
Because I-- I wore a big-- it's called a hustka, a big scarf, you know, like the farmers had there.
And I walked in.
And she, his wife-- he wasn't there.
Tanya ?] Fedorowich, his wife was there and she was making noodles.
You know, there you don't buy noodles.
You know, everything is homemade.
And she-- so I told her who I was, and I have to see the [NON-ENGLISH], which was a country doctor.
And she said, do you have a place where to cook?
And I always had-- I always said the opposite.
If they asked me if I have a place to cook, I said, of course.
And she says here, I'll give you-- and she gave me a whole thing of-- this macaroni that she was making.
Then her husband came.
And I told him, I says, things are bad.
My brothers, we ate nothing but onions.
He had to know what to do.
And he asked me, is everybody alive?
And I said, yes.
And he was a nice man, a very nice man.
And I said my brothers are-- they're dying.
They can't pass the water.
And he said, what are they eating?
And I told him, we ate onions.
He went in.
He had a little pantry, like a little thing.
And he-- I'll never forget-- whenever I look at a tin cup, I remember that.
I remember him and that story.
And he mixed up something.
I don't know what it was.
And believe it or not, it took away those welts, and my brothers got well.
And when I came back with that big thing of-- that she made for the-- my brother says he's gonna eat it raw.
And my mother says, if you eat it, my brother Morris, you'll die.
And he says, I'll take my chances.
He ate it.
He never died.
He ate the whole thing raw, just the way it was.
And so we didn't have enough food.
I start to go out, and my father would say go to this farm, or this farmer.
He'll never give you out.
So one day, it was wintertime.
It was so cold.
It was so cold my eyebrows were frozen.
And I had a little linen dress on, with a scarf around.
And I walked in the snow with galoshes.
And I came to Ossen.
His name was Ossen.
And he was very nice farmer.
He knew my father for years.
But I didn't know that his daughter had married a German collaborator, a policeman.
So, it was so cold.
And I came in the house.
And they were all sitting and eating.
And I see this uniform right in front of me.
His daughter, Olga, was pregnant.
She had married him.
And he must have seen the look on my face.
Because he got up.
He was a nice man.
He was a Ukrainian farmer.
And he knew me.
Because he knew me as a child, you know, as a little child.
And he said, sit down and eat.
I didn't know what to do.
I was so hungry and cold.
And the steam was coming out of everything.
And I remember there was a big thing of [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
So I ate.
I figured, well, at least-- I'll eat.
I'll be full before I die.
Then afterwards, and I had a glass of milk-- and after all that, he says, come, I want to talk to you.
And he took me, takes me into the pantry.
And the farmers there had everything dry for the winter.
Because, you know, they didn't have grocery stores like here.
He had dried apples, and dried this, and dried that.
He says, everybody alive?
And I said, yeah.
He says, how's your father?
And I says, well you know, considering.
He says, I'm sending this to your father.
And he sent him a little moonshine.
They were making their own whiskey.
He sent a bottle of whiskey, and a bar of soap, and bread, of course.
And I said, Ossen, you're giving me all that.
I'll never live-- I'll never live to get out, because the [NON-ENGLISH].
He says, yeah.
He's my son-in-law.
He married Olga.
He says, but this is my house.
And he took me outside, and I put the bag on my back.
And he crossed my-- the road, and he said, [NON-ENGLISH], go with God.
He crossed the road for me.
And as I left, I thought, I'll get-- I'll feel a bullet any minute in my back.
But God stepped in again.
These big snowflakes, as big as cotton balls, start to fall.
And it formed like a curtain.
And as I looked back to where he stood, Ossen, I couldn't see him no more.
That was another-- another day.
So then were able to get back to the barn safely--
--to the barn, right.
with all the food.
